competition.narrative: Thiess is running a blind 2-3 vendor competitive evaluation. The primary known competitor is Felix, deployed enterprise-wide by Thiess's parent company Cemic Group (Spanish conglomerate). Ryan stated 'we have appetite to use our own system if required or would have that conversation with our parent' (10 April, prior sweep), signaling that displacing Felix may require parent-level approval or negotiation—procurement sovereignty is not guaranteed. If Cemic mandates Felix for enterprise standardization, Thiess may lack authority to choose Zycus regardless of Felix's capability gaps. Beyond Felix, Ryan is gathering quotes from 2-3 unnamed competing vendors (22 June Next Step log), and no competitor names are known. The competitive shortlist is blind, so Zycus cannot tailor the win story to specific rival wedges. How we win: position Zycus as the mining-industry-fit platform with proven Australian mining customer references (Ryan's explicit ask), Oracle JDE integration depth, and Merlin AI differentiation (Merlin is in scope per Products field). The wedge is mining verticalization + AI negotiation/intake automation vs Felix's enterprise-generalist positioning. Activate it by delivering the Australian mining references Ryan requested and by running a Merlin AI use-case demo tailored to Thiess's procurement workflows before Ryan completes his evaluation in early July.

north_star_verdict.headline: Qualified deal 89 days from close is stalled in pre-proposal business case buildout with no Economic Buyer touch, no quantified business case, blind 2-3 vendor competitive evaluation underway, and parent company Felix deployment casting displacement authority shadow. Ryan reported no progress as of 2 July and timeline has already missed his own 30 June formal proposal target by 3 days.

flags: CO-AUTHOR THE BUSINESS CASE with Ryan before he completes his competitive evaluation in early July (couple of weeks from 22 June). He is building the case now and gathering competing quotes in parallel — this is the window to anchor Zycus as the AI-first, pain-replacing, referenced choice. Provide: (a) ROI benchmarking data (contract management efficiency, sourcing cycle time, AI-driven tactical procurement savings), (b) a peer Australian mining/resources customer win story (Oracle JDE, AI-led transformation), and (c) a draft executive summary Ryan can adapt for his CFO (Craig Morton) and Head of Procurement (Allan Styner) that frames Zycus as the safe, modern replacement for Felix. This move converts evaluation-mode engagement into committed championship and blocks competing vendors from out-referencing or out-quantifying Zycus.

SURFACE THE ECONOMIC BUYER (Craig Morton, CFO) before the business case goes to him cold. Ryan is the champion and is building the case, but Craig (EB, Executive Sponsor contact role) has not appeared on any call. The next gate after Ryan completes his business case is EB commercial and budget sign-off. An executive-to-CFO connect (the deal owner's manager to Craig, or a Zycus CFO/finance customer reference) that validates the ROI case and de-risks the platform decision — before Ryan formally submits the proposal — raises win probability and shortens the approval cycle. This is a $150k deal in Qualified stage with a 30 Sept close 96 days out; EB engagement now (early July, after Ryan's business case is ready) prevents a cold proposal and multi-round approval delays.

PROACTIVELY NAME THE COMPETING VENDORS and run a targeted competitive review by early July, before Ryan completes his evaluation. The shortlist is currently blind (Ryan gathering 2-3 quotes as of 22 June, no names mentioned). The likely peer set is Ivalua, Ariba, Coupa, GEP, or Jaggaer given the AI-first, enterprise S2P scope. If any of those have stronger Australian mining references or Oracle JDE stories than Zycus, they are a threat. Ask Ryan directly in the next conversation: 'Who else are you evaluating, and what are you benchmarking us against?' Then tailor the Zycus win story (AI depth, integration elegance, peer mining reference) to the specific competitive wedge for each named rival, and arm Ryan with a competitive comparison he can use in his business case. Blind competition is a coin-flip; named competition with a tailored wedge is winnable.

CO-AUTHOR THE BUSINESS CASE with Ryan before he completes his competitive evaluation in early July (couple of weeks from 22 June). He is building the case now and gathering competing quotes in parallel — this is the window to anchor Zycus as the AI-first, pain-replacing, referenced choice. Provide: (a) ROI benchmarking data (contract management efficiency, sourcing cycle time, AI-driven tactical procurement savings), (b) a peer Australian mining/resources customer win story (Oracle JDE, AI-led transformation), and (c) a draft executive summary Ryan can adapt for his CFO (Craig Morton) and Head of Procurement (Allan Styner) that frames Zycus as the safe, modern replacement for Felix. This move converts evaluation-mode engagement into committed championship and blocks competing vendors from out-referencing or out-quantifying Zycus.

SURFACE THE ECONOMIC BUYER (Craig Morton, CFO) before the business case goes to him cold. Ryan is the champion and is building the case, but Craig (EB, Executive Sponsor contact role) has not appeared on any call. The next gate after Ryan completes his business case is EB commercial and budget sign-off. An executive-to-CFO connect (the deal owner's manager to Craig, or a Zycus CFO/finance customer reference) that validates the ROI case and de-risks the platform decision — before Ryan formally submits the proposal — raises win probability and shortens the approval cycle. This is a $150k deal in Qualified stage with a 30 Sept close 96 days out; EB engagement now (early July, after Ryan's business case is ready) prevents a cold proposal and multi-round approval delays.

PROACTIVELY NAME THE COMPETING VENDORS and run a targeted competitive review by early July, before Ryan completes his evaluation. The shortlist is currently blind (Ryan gathering 2-3 quotes as of 22 June, no names mentioned). The likely peer set is Ivalua, Ariba, Coupa, GEP, or Jaggaer given the AI-first, enterprise S2P scope. If any of those have stronger Australian mining references or Oracle JDE stories than Zycus, they are a threat. Ask Ryan directly in the next conversation: 'Who else are you evaluating, and what are you benchmarking us against?' Then tailor the Zycus win story (AI depth, integration elegance, peer mining reference) to the specific competitive wedge for each named rival, and arm Ryan with a competitive comparison he can use in his business case. Blind competition is a coin-flip; named competition with a tailored wedge is winnable.
